sentences.text: I would like to mention-- before we arrived to Theresienstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was-- the protector of Bohemia at that time was a fellow by the name of SS man Heydrich.
And he was assassinated.
And the SS men wanted to-- to find out the people who assassinated him.
And they conducted search through every house.
And during that time, they also came to our house.
And when they entered the house, everybody had an identification pass.
And in there, the Jews had a big J in there, Juden, in-- in-- in the pass right next to the picture.
And as soon as they saw the J on that, the-- the SS men told my father to-- to come out of the house.
And this was at night.
This was already about 11 o'clock.
It was dark outside.
We didn't have any lights on the street.
So my father stepped-- stepped outside.
And they asked him if there is anybody in-- in the other side where I was sleeping with my grandmother.
This was a little-- little house back over our yard.
And there was a hallway and a staircase which led to my grandfather's house, apartment.
And so my father said, yeah, there is-- there is another building in the back.
And my-- only my mother lives there with my-- with my son.
So they-- they walked with him to the entrance.
And the SS man started to load his rifle.
And I soon as my father saw-- saw that he was loading his rifle, he started to run up the hallway, the stair-- staircase.
And there was no light in the-- in the-- on the staircase, so it was dark.
And as he-- as soon as he started to run, the SS men fired.
But my father seen him in the-- in the light of the starlight outside.
And he fell down on the steps.
So-- and as he was falling, his arm was in the air.
And-- and-- and he got hit through the-- through his arm.
And as soon as the SS men thought they killed him, they just walked away.
Well, my father got back.
And at night, he called his friend who was a doctor and asked him that he was shot and if he could come over there.
And so he-- he went over.
And the friend bandaged him up.
And he came home.
In the morning, all the sudden an SS man comes in a-- with a truck for the body.
And then when-- when they saw that he was alive, they started to laugh and just walked away.
Now, coming back to-- after that, not-- not long after that, we-- we went into-- into the transport.
And that's how he wind up in Theresienstadt.
When we came to Theresienstadt, my father became a blacksmith.
He got the job, and because he was pretty skill how to hoof.
The-- the man who was running the-- the blacksmith said, yeah, you-- you-- you-- you are a good man, so I'll keep you here.
And he was hoofing all the horses, which-- which were in this so-called [NON-ENGLISH], which is the agriculture section, and-- and also horses, riding horses which the SS men were riding.
One of the horse, I still remember.
It was a black horse, was belong to an SS man, Rahm, who was in charge of the-- of the camp later on.
I guess he was the last one.
And so I-- we-- we then got from this assembly barracks, we got an apartment-- an apartment, a house.
There were about-- about 12 people living in this room.
But it was-- at one time, it was a-- it was a house, which belonged to-- to the people who were moved out.
And I was with my father.
And-- and he was always going to work to that.
And I was doing all kinds of odd jobs, because I was only not even 13 at that time.
So-- and I got acquainted with a fellow by the name of [INAUDIBLE], who was in charge of the-- of the kids who were in these blocks.
They used to call them blockades.
And he says, you know, why don't guys-- why don't you get together a soccer team or something?
And we're going to form a league.
And so we formed a-- I got together with all the kids around I could in the area.
And we formed a-- we made a soccer team.
And we started to play around.
I used to be a good soccer player.
And we played against-- there was an area L417, which was for boys.
This was a home for boys.
And I wasn't-- you know, only a limited amount of kids could go into that.
I wasn't one of them.
But finally, because I was a good soccer player, one of the [INAUDIBLE] home-- home two, who was under the leadership of a fellow by the name of Joseph Taussig.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got-- I got there, and I started to play for them.
And-- and from there, I was transferred.
The kids who wanted me on the Home One, which was one of the better-- better homes.
And they wanted me to play for them, so I got transferred.
There was an opening in there.
Some of the guys went through the transport in-- into Auschwitz.
So I came into that Home One.
And that's where I stayed all the time until the home was dissolved when everybody was shipped out.
And I was the only one who stayed in the-- in the camp.
And what I did, I-- I-- then I had to get a job.
Most of the guys in the home, they work in the-- some of them were plumbers, repairing.
They had all kinds of odd jobs.
They worked in the fields, in the gardens.
And-- and I-- I wanted to-- I had an opportunity in the home to get some education.
Because the-- the-- the leaders, the guys who were in charge of these individuals previously were professors.
There was a Professor Eisinger who was-- who was from Moravia.
He was from Professor Swicker, who was from the University of [INAUDIBLE].
And another-- there was a Professor Cohn, who was a mathematician.
So these guys said, well, you know, you guys let's get take a chance.
And we're going to start a schools.
So we-- we had-- some of the kids in the morning went to work.
They had school in the afternoon.
Some of them went to work in the afternoon.
They-- they had classes in the morning.

And any time we had some kind of a visit from-- from the SS man, and so you know everybody-- there was always a guard outside the way watching, so nobody would-- so we wouldn't get surprised or the books wouldn't be visible.
Because we couldn't-- we could--
These were secret schools, yeah.
Nobody--
No, they weren't permitted.
These were secret schools.
But [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and so that's how we got some education.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on, I got acquainted with a-- with a-- with a professor who claimed-- he told me that he was from-- from Switzerland.
He was together with Einstein at the University in-- in-- in Switzerland.
His name was Noel.
And I used to-- oh, oh, oh, I got a job as a driving horses.
First, I drove really oxen.
And the reason why I got this job, because my-- one of my uncles was a skilled rider.
And he also knew something about agriculture.
And what he would do, he would exercise all horses for the SS men.
And-- and he also would be in charge of this-- this [NON-ENGLISH], this-- this section.
So he gave me a pair of horse-- a pair of oxen first.
And I drove the oxen.
And I alone, I-- I was advanced to become a driver of the horses.
I had a pair of horses, and I was driving with the horses.
I-- my assignments were to go plow the fields outside the-- the camp.
I would have always a a pass.
For-- for example, if a women, if they had to plow potatoes, so the women had to pick the potatoes, I would be plowing.
They would be walking behind, picking up the potatoes and loading it on the wagon.
Then I would bring it into the-- into the ghetto and into different kitchens in-- in these different barracks.
And-- or I would be cutting-- cutting grass for hay or bringing hay from outside.
Because there were barns outside the town in the field.
And we would be bringing it inside for the-- for the horses.

I also had an assignment once a week, and later sometimes twice a week, to bring people, dead people, to first to-- to-- to-- they were buried outside the camp, and then to the crematory-- when the crematorium was built, to the-- to the crematorium.
Well, the people-- a lot of people were dying of old age.
A lot of people were dying from starvation.
Because especially younger-- older people, they didn't have enough-- enough to eat, also, from disease.
There was typhoid also broke out in-- in a camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so that was most the people were dying.
I lost my grandmother there, because this was of age.
I lost also my aunt.
She had pneumonia.
And there were no-- very few drugs there, so she just died from there.
Otherwise, she would have been saved.
I remember when-- when the typhoid broke out, there was-- you couldn't get inoculated.
So what happened, somebody suggested that you should get blood from your parents.
Because they have, as a kid, you know, the parents have developed already more immunity to different-- different diseases.
So you would get a-- one time, I got a-- my father gave me his blood.
And I got a shot of his blood.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So this is what I was-- I spent most of my time in this-- in this [INAUDIBLE].
I had to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning, feed the horses.
And because I was able to go outside, I had access to-- to potatoes or sometimes tomatoes.
And I would sneak all this thing into town and trade it for-- with guys who were working in the butchers shops.
I would give him potatoes.
They would give me a piece of meat and stuff like that.
And then my father was working in this blacksmith.
And I would bring it over there to to him, and we would cook it there.
He was-- later on, he was able to make out of a coal bin a-- a-- little-- little apartment, which had a a bunk.
And we moved-- we moved in later on.
But my father lived at a different place originally.
And my mother lived together with my sister.
My mother lived in a-- in a house, which was assigned the number L410.
The-- the-- the streets were divided into L and Qs.
I think that's-- that was it.
Yes.
Yes.
Inside the town, you were able to-- to-- to move-- move around.
There was no-- I-- I didn't feel any restriction of-- but, you know, I seen--
Really, truly a ghetto, yes.
It-- the-- the town really served for show-- to showing the outside world that the Jews are not really mistreated.
As you-- You know, at one time, they spruced up the center of the town.
They had a Jewish band played every-- every, you know, every week some classical music.
It was a good, good band.
Also, there were shows there.
I-- I remember in the home where I was, they played [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] My-- the-- the guy who was in charge, he sang in that.
And the-- the kids, they were also participated in-- in shows.
They had a cabarets.
You know, Jewish writers wrote a lot of cabarets.
And these guy participated.
They-- it was attended mostly by Jewish people.
But when the Swiss Red Cross, for example, came in, they would be-- you know, try to show them how well the Jews are living.
They-- they-- they went several-- they came several times.
I recall one time, I was-- I was assigned to a group of kids that we were showing how good we eat.
They were giving us cookies.
And-- and-- and we were telling Uncle Rahm, you know, the SS man, we never-- when we-- we couldn't-- we used to call him Uncle Rahm.
We had to call him Uncle Rahm.
And so that was-- that was to the extent it was always.
And in the meantime, only once in a while, always there were transports coming out from-- from the ghetto to-- to Auschwitz.
Well, at first, very few people knew what-- what it was to-- to-- to-- to go to Auschwitz.
But later on, you know, people got-- somehow the news got in.
On top of it, I recall there was a German guy who came.
He was in his-- in his 20s.
And what happened is that he was-- he was an SS man.
He was a Jew-- he was a Jewish guy.
But he was an SS man.
What happened is father was Jewish, and his mother was Christian.
His father died when he was very young, and he never knew his father.
His mother never told him about-- about his father.
And he was-- she remarried.
And somehow, the Germans, when they were checking through his-- through his ancestors, they found out that his father, that he is really Jewish.
And he came from the Eastern Zone.
So he was telling us about all these thing what-- what-- what is happening.
And though that was my personal experience.
But of most of the older people, I was from 13 to 16, when I was-- I had my bar mitzvah also in the-- in the camp.
I remember Rabbi [INAUDIBLE] conducted the bar mitzvah.
It was in the-- in the Hanover barracks on-- [POINTING] right under the roof.
They build up a little-- a little temple, which was really just benches.
And-- and-- and the Orthodox Jews used to come over, they're mostly older-- older people.
And I had my bar mitzvah.
And they gave me the wrong chapter.
And about two days before, he told me, you know, he made a mistake.
So you had to-- you have to do-- I didn't do too well reciting it.
But [LAUGHS] anyway, I had my bar mitzvah there.
The only-- the only-- | remember one time there was a flood of the Oder River, which was flowing outside-- outside the Theresienstadt.
And I was-- we were directed to-- we had some barns there.
We were directed to bring the-- the hay into town to the-- into our place.
So I went over there.
And SS men approached me.
And I-- I had some women who were loading up the hay from there.
And they were on the wagon, because we were going through-- through water.
Well, when the SS men came around, they didn't want to be in that water.
So they directed me to-- to take them over this-- this this big-- big puddle.
So they chased all the women from the-- from the wagon into the water.
I had to jump off into the water and just to walk next to the horses and take them over-- over this-- this flooded area.
And otherwise, there were punishments also.
There were some guys who tried to escape.
They caught them.
And then they marched them through the-- there was also a public hanging in-- in the-- in the camp.
Because these people escape.
Or, they traded cigarettes, and they were caught with cigarettes by the SS men.
Cigarettes were-- were forbidden in the camp.
And-- and they-- they hung them.
They-- they publicly hung them so-- as an example that people, you know, shouldn't [INAUDIBLE].
No.
I have not seen that.
No.
I didn't see them, but I know-- it you know, it-- it happened.
Because what happened where my-- where my cousin lived, the guy who hanged them was a Jewish guy.
A Jewish guy had to hang them.
As a matter of fact, he said one guy-- the-- the-- the rope broke, and he should have gotten clemency.
But the SS men shot him on it.
So I know that it happened, because he told us this story.
So-- and he lived with my-- in these barracks which was, as I said, these were-- at one time, maybe hundred soldiers lived in these-- in-- on this one-- one floor.
My mother-- yeah.
My mother was-- was a nurse.
She was-- she was in-- in a hospital.
And my sister was working in the fields.
She was just going in the fields.
She was living with my mother.
And she didn't get any education in there, because there was no opportunity for her in-- in these-- in these houses to-- to get any education.
So she was just working in the fields.
I-- because of-- you know, I had the horses.
So I had access.
I could drive through the town, outside the town with a pass.
And I was-- when I went outside, I had a-- I would be-- a gendarme would be assigned to me.
And he would guard as he would walk with us.
Some of the gendarmes were friendly.
Some of them also were not friendly.
Some of the-- I did all kinds of things over there sometimes.
Because I had these women, young girls.
And, you know, sometimes the guys wanted to get acquainted with some of the girls.
So I would try to get them together.
I would be like pimping and trying to-- to get them together.
As a matter of fact, one of the girl after the war married one of the gendarme.
And she-- she came back.
She was-- she stayed in the camp.
So some of them were friendly.
Some of them were not-- not that friendly.
I-- I haven't had too many SS men assigned to-- to me to-- to-- to-- to guard us.
Because I don't think too many-- too many people fled-- fled from-- there was one time they-- they had a census of-- of everybody in the-- in the camp.
So we had everybody had to go to this one field.
And they counted everybody.
So they make sure that everybody is in place as was one time they made a census.
I-- I saw my mother maybe-- maybe twice, three times a week, yes.
Well, my father I seen, because I always could have--
My-- my father?
Maybe about the same time.
He would-- you know, after work in the evening, he would go out.
And also, my father was working with this Christian guy who was living outside.
And this guy was a pretty good guy.
He was not a-- he was just a regular Czech fellow who was a blacksmith.
They needed him.
And he was harmless.
And he would sometimes bring him something from outside.
And-- and so--
OK.
In-- in this camp were only-- only Jews.
There were all kinds of nationalities in there.
At first, there were Czech, mostly Czech fellows there.
Then later on, people from Germany came there.
And some of the Jews, surprisingly, you know, they thought they going to a-- to a recreation place.
And they brought in all kinds of things.
And they confiscated it.
The Germans confiscated it.
And later on, they came out with ghetto money.
And you were able to buy with that-- depending what kind of a job you had.
So they were paid so much, so much of this money.
And you were able to buy somebody else's.
[LAUGHS] They made up phony stores, you know, stores for show, for the visits of these Swiss-- Swiss teams.
And you would be buying somebody else's stuff, all the stuff.
But at least you were buying something which you needed.
And-- and some of the German people, very old, very old, most of them were older people, they would be bringing in [SIGHS] all kinds of things.
Naturally, jewelry they would confiscate.
But clothing and stuff like that they would be putting into these stores.
Then later on, some of-- some Hungarian Jews came in.
Some Dutch Jews came in.
And that was about-- about all the-- the nationalities.
Then when there were-- at towards the end of the war, they were bringing in death march.
Some of these trains came in with people who were in death march.
They were bringing them as-- as the Russians were advancing.
So they were bringing people completely decimated.
I remember I had to pick up peels from these Hanover barracks.
In-- in the basement, the potatoes were always stored in-- in-- in the cellars of these big barracks.
And people would be-- women would be peeling potatoes for the kitchens there.
And the peels, I would bring a wagon there.
And they would throw these peels into these wagons.
And I would take it into the field as a compost.
But when these people-- these people came for this-- from these death marches, they brought them into these-- into these barracks.
And I came to pick up these peels.
And as I came out from these-- from this cellar with the-- with the horses up, all of a sudden, everybody started to run towards those peels.
And I was afraid that I'm going to run somebody over.
So I just stopped, took the horses out, left the wagon there.
And-- and when I came back in an hour, all the rotten peels and all the peels just disappeared.
People just ate it, because they were from death march.
They-- they were just hungry.
I picked up the empty wagon and just went-- went home.
And-- Were there other sections of the camp, particularly toward the end of the war, where there were non-Jews, or did it stay Jewish?
No, it stayed Jewish.
There was outsiders.
I said, outside of camp, there was a-- this [NON-ENGLISH], the little-- little fortress, which was political prisoners.
There were also some Jews in there, but they were politically-- politically-- they were captured for some political reasons or for-- for no reason at all, sometimes.
But they were over there.
That-- I went into that place only once.
And it was at the end of the war when they started-- the Germans-- started to shoot some of these prisoners.
And they were over there.
And this was in May.
And I had to bring my wagon, a flat, flat wagon.
And they would put this-- these wagon-- these bodies were already in-- they were in the-- in the sun.
And they started to-- to-- you know, started to decompose.
So they would put Lyme on it.
And I would take these to the crematorium.
And also, when typhoid broke out, what happened, we had to-- I was afraid.
Because lice used to transform typhoid.
So I would make my-- my-- the harnesses longer, so I wouldn't have to sit on the wagon when these bodies were.
Because they were-- some of them died from typhoid.
So I'd be walking next to the wagon and just far away from wagon, so I wouldn't get-- catch any lice to get typhoid.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that's--
Yes.
[SIGHS] Towards the end, we were-- we weren't sure whether we're going to survive.
Because everybody-- my friends-- all my friends disappeared.
I was the only-- only one left.
And what happened at the end, they-- they closed the home.
Because there was nobody-- a couple kids were left over.
So I moved with my father into a-- and we just weren't sure whether we're going to survive.
That we just expected, you know, one of these days, we'll be also in a-- in a transport.
But we always watched for American planes.
And-- and because the American planes were flying around.
You could hear the-- the thunder.
And-- and so we were saying, well, maybe it's-- all these different news were coming in.
And also, my father was getting some of the news from this fellow who he worked with.
While I was in the-- in-- in the home, we were making out a magazine.
I guess I should--
OK.
OK.
